claims.evidence_text: A harvest objective with current varieties grown in Virginia should be 60-70 heads/sq. ft. with at least 30 kernels/head.
claims.claim_summary: Harvest objective for Virginia winter wheat is 60-70 heads/sq ft with at least 30 kernels/head.

claims.evidence_text: A harvest objective with current varieties grown in Virginia should be 60-70 heads/sq. ft. with at least 30 kernels/head.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat harvest objective is at least 30 kernels per head.

claims.evidence_text: a moderate amount (15-30 lbs. of N/acre) is all that is needed for establishing a timely planted winter wheat crop.
claims.claim_summary: A moderate fall nitrogen rate of 15-30 lb N/acre is sufficient to establish a timely-planted winter wheat crop.

claims.evidence_text: Total spring N applications (growth stage 25 plus growth stage 30) should not exceed a total of 120 lbs. N/acre in order to avoid problems with lodging and yield loss. ... Up to 120 lbs. N/acre may be applied at growth stage 30
claims.claim_summary: Spring nitrogen for winter wheat is split between GS25 (tiller-based) and GS30 (tissue-based), with total spring N not exceeding 120 lb N/acre to avoid lodging.

claims.evidence_text: Total spring N applications (growth stage 25 plus growth stage 30) should not exceed a total of 120 lbs. N/acre in order to avoid problems with lodging and yield loss.
claims.claim_summary: Total spring nitrogen for winter wheat should not exceed 120 lb N/acre to avoid lodging and yield loss.

claims.evidence_text: Excessive plant-available N produces wheat plants that are susceptible to lodging and disease with resulting decreased yields ... Excessive N applications during the early spring tillering phase can result in spindly plants that are more likely to lodge
claims.claim_summary: Excessive nitrogen makes winter wheat susceptible to lodging and disease, especially when applied during early spring tillering.

claims.evidence_text: If tiller numbers are low, 50/sq. ft. or less, N fertilization at this time is critical for the crop to develop any reasonable yield potential. If tiller numbers are high, 100/sq. ft. or more, no N application is needed at this time.
claims.claim_summary: At spring greenup (GS25), winter wheat tiller density of 50/sq ft or less requires N, while 100/sq ft or more needs none.

claims.evidence_text: Eight years of research in Virginia has shown that tissue N content at growth stage 30 (GS 30) is a reliable indicator of how much additional N fertilizer is needed
claims.claim_summary: Tissue nitrogen content at GS30 is a reliable indicator of additional N fertilizer needs for winter wheat.

claims.evidence_text: Foliar applications (10 to 20 lbs. N/acre) of urea at this growth stage have been shown to increase grain protein but not yield.
claims.claim_summary: Foliar urea (10-20 lb N/acre) at flowering increases winter wheat grain protein but not yield.
